TL;DR: This paper addresses the issue of correlated speckle noise in multi-look complex holography and proposes a reconstruction framework based on a first-order Markov assumption for robust performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-look acquisition suppresses speckle noise, but conventional methods degrade due to inter-look correlation caused by practical hardware constraints.

Method: The proposed method models speckle as a first-order Markov process, derives a constrained maximum likelihood estimation framework, and uses projected gradient descent with deep image priors and scalable Monte Carlo techniques.

Result: Simulation results show robust reconstruction under strong inter-look correlations, outperforming existing methods and approaching ideal independent-look performance.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling inter-look correlation is important for holographic reconstruction, offering a practical solution for realistic conditions, with code provided for reproducibility.

Abstract: Multi-look acquisition is a widely used strategy for reducing speckle noise in coherent imaging systems such as digital holography. By acquiring multiple measurements, speckle can be suppressed through averaging or joint reconstruction, typically under the assumption that speckle realizations across looks are statistically independent. In practice, however, hardware constraints limit measurement diversity, leading to inter-look correlation that degrades the performance of conventional methods. In this work, we study the reconstruction of speckle-free reflectivity from complex-valued multi-look measurements in the presence of correlated speckle. We model the inter-look dependence using a first-order Markov process and derive the corresponding likelihood under a first-order Markov approximation, resulting in a constrained maximum likelihood estimation problem. To solve this problem, we develop an efficient projected gradient descent framework that combines gradient-based updates with implicit regularization via deep image priors, and leverages Monte Carlo approximation and matrix-free operators for scalable computation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach remains robust under strong inter-look correlation, achieving performance close to the ideal independent-look scenario and consistently outperforming methods that ignore such dependencies. These results highlight the importance of explicitly modeling inter-look correlation and provide a practical framework for multi-look holographic reconstruction under realistic acquisition conditions. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Computational-Imaging-RU/MLE-Holography-Markov.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [422] [Decision-Focused Federated Learning Under Heterogeneous Objectives and Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20031)
*Konstantinos Ziliaskopoulos,Alexander Vinel*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper introduces Decision-Focused Federated Learning (DFFL), emphasizing how heterogeneity in clients' optimization problems affects predictive models in a federated learning setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address federated learning scenarios where clients have diverse optimization problems and no raw data sharing is permitted.

Method: The authors utilize the SPO+ surrogate loss and establish heterogeneity bounds using support function representations and hybrid norms. They propose a heuristic rule for exploring when federation improves decision-making.

Result: Federation performs robustly in strongly convex problems even under heterogeneity, but constraint variability degrades performance in polyhedral problems, particularly for clients with many samples.

Conclusion: Federated learning using SPO+ and FedAvg shows promise in strongly convex optimization contexts, and requires careful assessment of heterogeneity penalties to ensure effectiveness.

Abstract: We consider what we refer to as {Decision-Focused Federated Learning (DFFL)} framework, i.e., a predict-then-optimize approach employed by a collection of agents, where each agent's predictive model is an input to a downstream linear optimization problem, and no direct exchange of raw data is allowed. Importantly, clients can differ both in objective functions and in feasibility constraints. We build on the well-known SPO+ approach and develop heterogeneity bounds for the SPO+ surrogate loss in this case. This is accomplished by employing a support function representation of the feasible region, separating (i) objective shift via norm distances between the cost vectors and (ii) feasible-set shift via shape distances between the constraint sets. In the case of strongly convex feasible regions, sharper bounds are derived due to the optimizer stability. Building on these results, we define a heuristic local-versus-federated excess risk decision rule which, under SPO+ risk, gives a condition for when federation can be expected to improve decision quality: the heterogeneity penalty must be smaller than the statistical advantage of pooling data. We implement a FedAvg-style DFFL set of experiments on both polyhedral and strongly convex problems and show that federation is broadly robust in the strongly convex setting, while performance in the polyhedral setting degrades primarily with constraint heterogeneity, especially for clients with many samples. In other words, especially for the strongly convex case, an approach following a direct implementation of FedAvg and SPO+ can still yield promising performance even when the downstream optimization problems are noticeably different.

</details>


### [423] [Robust Out-of-Distribution Stochastic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20147)
*Xianyu Li,Huan Xu,Xiaolin Huang,Chao Shang*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework for decision-making under uncertainty without prior access to the target distribution, using related data distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of prior access to target distribution data in decision-making under uncertainty, leveraging related distributions for robust out-of-distribution performance.

Method: The approach constructs a data-driven uncertainty set in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) based on related data distributions, and integrates it into a min-max stochastic optimization program.

Result: Rigorous generalization guarantees are provided for the uncertainty set and solution. Numerical experiments on real-world problems like portfolio optimization validate its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The proposed framework delivers robust out-of-distribution decision-making, even with limited related data, enhanced by theoretical guarantees and practical performance.

Abstract: Data-driven decision-making under uncertainty typically presumes the collection of historical data from an unknown target probability distribution. However, one may have no access to any data from the target distribution prior to decision-making. To address this challenge, we propose robust out-of-distribution stochastic optimization, a novel data-driven framework that effectively utilizes relevant data distributions for robust decision-making under unseen distributions. A key feature of our framework is that all data distributions are assumed to be randomly generated from a meta-distribution over distributions. To describe uncertainty in distribution generation, we propose to learn a data-driven uncertainty set in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) from relevant data distributions, with adjustable conservatism. We then incorporate this set into a min-max stochastic program to derive robust decisions. Notably, under randomness of distribution generation, we establish rigorous out-of-distribution generalization guarantees for the uncertainty set as well as the solution. To ease problem-solving in RKHS, an approximate parametrization with a provably bounded suboptimality and a row generation strategy are presented. Extensive numerical experiments on multi-item newsvendor and portfolio optimization demonstrate the superior out-of-distribution performance of our decision-making framework under unseen data distribution, even when only a small or moderate number of relevant sources are available.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [424] [Toward designing workload-aware Surface Code Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19855)
*Archisman Ghosh,Avimita Chatterjee,Swaroop Ghosh*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new quantum computing architecture to optimize fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) by balancing qubit accessibility and density, resulting in reduced resource requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FTQC architectures face trade-offs between qubit overhead and workload latency, motivating the need for a balanced design to improve practical quantum computing efficiency.

Method: The authors propose a surface-code architecture that centralizes ancilla qubits for uniform access, introduces a $T$-gate workload-driven floorplan, optimizes $Y$-gate latencies, and supports concurrent execution of multiple programs.

Result: The approach reduces data tile requirements by ~21%, optimizes cycles per instruction near the ideal regime, and achieves ~90% efficiency in concurrent executions.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture significantly improves resource utilization and flexibility in FTQC, making it a step closer to practical quantum computing advantages.

Abstract: Practical quantum advantage is expected to depend on fault-tolerant quantum computing, although the architectural overhead needed to support fault tolerance is still extremely high. Prior FTQC designs generally emphasize either fast logical-qubit accessibility at the cost of significant qubit overhead, or high logical-qubit density at the cost of added workload latency. We propose an architecture that balances these competing objectives by placing surface-code patches around an ancilla-centric region, which yields nearly uniform ancilla access for all data qubits. Building on this design, we introduce a new workload-driven placement method that uses the $T$-gate profile of an application to determine an effective floorplan. We further provide a reconfigurable optimization for reducing the latency of $Y$-gate measurements on a per-workload basis. To improve flexibility, we also study concurrent execution of multiple programs on the same architecture. Numerical evaluation indicates that our approach keeps cycles per instruction near the optimal regime while reducing the number of required data tiles by up to $\sim21\%$, and achieves up to $\sim90\%$ efficiency when running 10 programs concurrently.

</details>


### [425] [Co-Designing Error Mitigation and Error Detection for Logical Qubits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19871)
*Rohan S. Kumar,Takahiro Tsunoda,Sophia H. Xue,Dantong Li,Robert J. Schoelkopf,Yongshan Ding*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper explores integrating Quantum Error Detection (QED) and Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC), showing potential for significant error reduction while analyzing architectural and design challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the unexplored complementary cost profiles of QED and PEC to enhance error management efficiency in near-term quantum workloads.

Method: The authors derive an efficiency condition for optimal QED intervals, evaluate Iceberg codes, and introduce the steady-state extraction protocol to mitigate PEC accuracy degradation caused by transient error profiles.

Result: Using the $[[6,4,2]]$ Iceberg code for QAOA, PEC+QED achieves 2-11x lower absolute error and up to 31x lower mean squared error compared to PEC alone, significantly improving error rates with compounding benefits.

Conclusion: Optimizing QED intervals and integrating steady-state extraction with PEC effectively combines the strengths of QED and PEC, achieving substantial error reductions in quantum computing tasks.

Abstract: Near-term quantum workloads demand error management, yet the two lightest-weight techniques, Quantum Error Detection (QED) and Probabilistic Error Cancellation (PEC), have complementary cost profiles whose joint architectural design space remains unexplored. QED encodes logical qubits and discards error-flagged runs, filtering noise with low qubit overhead but leaving residual errors; PEC can correct these in software, but at exponential cost in noise strength. If QED efficiently reduces per-gate noise, PEC's cost savings can outweigh QED's discard overhead; realizing this, however, requires solving two system-level design challenges.
  First, the \textit{QED interval} -- how often detection cycles are inserted -- is a tunable architectural parameter governing the cost-accuracy tradeoff. We derive an efficiency condition and show that the canonical one-cycle-per-gate frequency does not achieve break-even in any code we evaluate, while optimized intervals on high-rate Iceberg codes do. Second, we discover that naive PEC+QED integration \textit{degrades} accuracy below the QED-only baseline. The root cause is a transient error profile in the first detection cycle that corrupts PEC's noise model. We develop \textit{steady-state extraction}, a co-designed characterization protocol that isolates steady-state error behavior, reducing estimation bias by up to $10.2\times$. On a $[[6,4,2]]$ Iceberg code running QAOA ($p{=}4$--$8$) with a fixed shot budget, PEC+QED achieves $2$--$11\times$ lower absolute error and up to $31\times$ lower MSE versus PEC on physical qubits, with per-interval savings compounding over interval depth.

</details>


### [426] [Distributed Quantum Optimization for Large-Scale Higher-Order Problems with Dense Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20599)
*Seongmin Kim,Vincent R. Pascuzzi,Travis S. Humble,Thomas Beck,Sanghyo Hwang,Tengfei Luo,Eungkyu Lee,In-Saeng Suh*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The study introduces a distributed quantum optimization framework (DQOF) for large-scale higher-order optimization (HUBO) problems, outperforming classical methods and demonstrating promising scalability for near-term quantum hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solving higher-order optimization problems (HUBOs) is highly challenging with classical methods due to dense, multi-variable interactions and constraints in scaling solutions.

Method: The paper develops DQOF, a framework that uses quantum circuits to represent higher-order interactions while integrating high-performance computing for large-scale parallelism, and clustering to enable wide quantum circuits without increasing their depth.

Result: The framework successfully solves HUBOs with up to 500 variables within 170 seconds and achieves better results in both solution quality and scalability compared to classical methods, with demonstrated applications in the practical problem of optical metamaterial design.

Conclusion: DQOF is established as a scalable and practical solution for tackling large-scale and complex optimization problems, leveraging quantum computing advancements effectively.

Abstract: Many real-world problems are naturally formulated as higher-order optimization (HUBO) tasks involving dense, multi-variable interactions, which are challenging to solve with classical methods. Quantum optimization offers a promising route, but hardware constraints and limitations to quadratic formulations have hampered their practicality. Here, we develop a distributed quantum optimization framework (DQOF) for dense, large-scale HUBO problems. DQOF assigns quantum circuits a central role in directly capturing higher-order interactions, while high-performance computing orchestrates large-scale parallelism and coordination. A clustering strategy enables wide quantum circuits without increasing depth, allowing efficient execution on near-term quantum hardware. We demonstrate high-quality solutions for HUBOs up to 500 variables within 170 seconds, significantly outperforming conventional approaches in solution quality and scalability. Applied to optical metamaterial design, DQOF efficiently discovers high-performance structures and shows that higher-order interactions are important for practical optimization problems. These results establish DQOF as a practical and scalable computational paradigm for large-scale scientific optimization.

</details>


### [427] [Distributed Quantum-Enhanced Optimization: A Topographical Preconditioning Approach for High-Dimensional Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20639)
*Dominik Soós,Marc Paterno,John Stenger,Nikos Chrisochoides*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces D-QEO, a framework combining quantum and classical computing for efficient optimization of large-scale problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge that optimization problems become intractable as the number of variables grows due to the exponential increase in search space, where classical and current quantum methods face limitations.

Method: D-QEO uses a quantum processor (QPU) as a topographical preconditioner to identify promising regions in the search space, which are then refined by a classical GPU-accelerated solver. Separable functions are exploited to divide the problem into smaller sub-problems for efficient concurrent execution.

Result: Benchmarks on 10-dimensional Rastrigin and Ackley functions show D-QEO mitigates exponential failure rates observed in classical methods. It also reduces iterations for classical solvers to converge.

Conclusion: D-QEO demonstrates a practical method to leverage near-term quantum resources in optimization tasks, overcoming scaling issues and enhancing classical solver efficiency.

Abstract: Optimization problems become fundamentally challenging as the number of variables increases. Because the volume of the search space grows exponentially, classical algorithms frequently fail to locate the global minimum of non-convex functions. While quantum optimization offers a potential alternative, mapping continuous problems onto near-term quantum hardware introduces severe scaling limits and barren plateaus. To bridge this gap, we propose the Distributed Quantum-Enhanced Optimization (D-QEO) framework. Instead of forcing the quantum processor to find the exact minimum, we use it simply as a topographical preconditioner. The QPU maps the landscape to locate the most promising basin of attraction, generating high-quality seed points for a classical GPU-accelerated solver to refine. To make this approach viable for utility-scale problems, we exploit the mathematical structure of separable functions. This allows us to cut a 50-qubit (i.e., $2^{50}$) global search space into independent and manageable sub-spaces using 5-qubit subcircuits. By executing these fragments concurrently with CUDA-Q, we completely bypass the overhead of cross-register entanglement and classical tensor knitting for separable functions. Benchmarks on the 10-dimensional Rastrigin and Ackley functions show that D-QEO prevents the exponential failure rates observed in purely classical algorithms. Furthermore, this quantum warm-start significantly reduces the number of classical BFGS iterations required to converge, providing a highly practical blueprint for utilizing near-term quantum resources in complex global search.

</details>


### [428] [Option Pricing on Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Computers: A Quantum Neural Network Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19832)
*Sebastian Zając,Rafał Pracht*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper explores using Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) within the Black-Scholes-Merton framework for option pricing, and evaluates their performance on various quantum hardware platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for accurate and efficient pricing models in the global derivatives market, and to explore the potential of quantum machine learning in this domain.

Method: A fully quantum approach using 2-qubit Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) was implemented and tested on multiple quantum processors like IBM Fez, IQM Garnet, IonQ Forte, and Rigetti Ankaa-3.

Result: The QNN approach provided accurate pricing approximations consistently across different quantum hardware despite limitations of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices.

Conclusion: QNNs are a viable method for derivative pricing and hold potential for application in more complex financial models beyond the Black-Scholes-Merton framework.

Abstract: In a global derivatives market with notional values in the hundreds of trillions of dollars, the accuracy and efficiency of pricing models are of fundamental importance, with direct implications for risk management, capital allocation, and regulatory compliance. In this work, we employ the Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) framework not as an end in itself, but as a controlled benchmark environment in which to rigorously assess the capabilities of quantum machine learning methods.
  We propose a fully quantum approach to option pricing based on Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs), and, to the best of our knowledge, present one of the first implementations of such a methodology on currently available quantum hardware. Specifically, we investigate whether QNNs, by exploiting the geometric structure of Hilbert space, can effectively approximate option pricing functions.
  Our implementation utilizes a compact 2-qubit QNN architecture evaluated across multiple state-of-the-art quantum processors, including IBM Fez, IQM Garnet, IonQ Forte, and Rigetti Ankaa-3. This cross-platform study reveals distinct hardware-dependent performance characteristics while demonstrating that accurate pricing approximations can be achieved consistently across different devices despite the constraints of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) hardware.
  The results provide empirical evidence that QNN-based approaches constitute a viable framework for derivative pricing. While the analysis is conducted within the BSM setting, the broader significance lies in the potential extension of these methods to more realistic and computationally demanding models, including local volatility, stochastic volatility, and interest rate frameworks commonly used in practice.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.str-el'></div>

# cond-mat.str-el [[Back]](#toc)

### [429] [Gauge-Equivariant Graph Neural Networks for Lattice Gauge Theories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20797)
*Ali Rayat,Yaohang Li,Gia-Wei Chern*

Main category: cond-mat.str-el

TL;DR: The paper introduces a gauge-equivariant graph neural network designed to handle local gauge symmetries in machine learning, addressing existing limitations around learning nonlocal observables in systems with site-dependent symmetries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a principled framework for machine learning to accommodate local gauge symmetries and effectively handle nonlocal observables, given their prominence in fundamental physics and quantum systems.

Method: The authors propose a gauge-equivariant graph neural network that implements matrix-valued, gauge-covariant features in message passing, enabling symmetry-compatible updates and natural emergence of loop-like structures via local operations.

Result: The method was validated across different regimes (pure gauge, gauge-matter, and dynamical), demonstrating its capability to capture nonlocal correlations and work effectively under local symmetries.

Conclusion: Gauge-equivariant message passing offers a robust paradigm for studying systems governed by local symmetries, extending machine learning's applicability to more complex quantum systems and interactions.

Abstract: Local gauge symmetry underlies fundamental interactions and strongly correlated quantum matter, yet existing machine-learning approaches lack a general, principled framework for learning under site-dependent symmetries, particularly for intrinsically nonlocal observables. Here we introduce a gauge-equivariant graph neural network that embeds non-Abelian symmetry directly into message passing via matrix-valued, gauge-covariant features and symmetry-compatible updates, extending equivariant learning from global to fully local symmetries. In this formulation, message passing implements gauge-covariant transport across the lattice, allowing nonlocal correlations and loop-like structures to emerge naturally from local operations. We validate the approach across pure gauge, gauge-matter, and dynamical regimes, establishing gauge-equivariant message passing as a general paradigm for learning in systems governed by local symmetry.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [430] [Worst-Case Optimal GPU Datalog](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20073)
*Yihao Sun,Kunting Qi,Thomas Gilray,Sidharth Kumar,Kristopher Micinski*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: SRDatalog introduces a novel GPU-based Datalog engine using Worst-Case Optimal Joins (WCOJ) to avoid memory and performance issues in traditional GPU Datalog engines that rely on binary joins, achieving significant speedups in program analysis workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GPU Datalog engines face limitations with binary joins, leading to inefficiencies in handling complex multi-way queries and potential memory overflows under the AGM bound.

Method: SRDatalog implements WCOJ with flat columnar storage, deterministic memory allocation, root-level histogram-guided load balancing, structural helper-relation splitting, and stream-aligned rule multiplexing to optimize efficiency and mitigate hardware inefficiencies.

Result: SRDatalog achieves geometric-mean speedups ranging from 21x to 47x on real-world program-analysis workloads when compared to existing GPU Datalog engines.

Conclusion: SRDatalog successfully addresses the limitations of binary join-based GPU Datalog systems, offering better scalability and performance for complex workloads by effectively utilizing GPU architecture and WCOJ.

Abstract: Datalog is a declarative logic-programming language used for complex analytic reasoning workloads such as program analysis and graph analytics. Datalog's popularity is due to its unique price-point, marrying logic-defined specification with the potential for massive data parallelism. While traditional engines are CPU-based, the memory-bound nature of Datalog has led to increasing interest in leveraging GPUs. These engines beat CPU-based engines by operationalizing iterated relational joins via SIMT-friendly join algorithms. Unfortunately, all existing GPU Datalog engines are built on binary joins, which are inadequate for the complex multi-way queries arising in production systems such as DOOP and ddisasm. For these queries, binary decomposition can incur the AGM bound asymptotic blowup in time and space, leading to OOM failures regardless of join order. Worst-Case Optimal Joins (WCOJ) avoid this blowup, but their attribute-at-a-time intersections map poorly to SIMT hardware under key skew, causing severe load imbalance across Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs). We present SRDatalog, the first GPU Datalog engine based on WCOJ. SRDatalog uses flat columnar storage and two-phase deterministic memory allocation to avoid the OOM failures of binary joins and the index-rebuild overheads of static WCOJ systems. To mitigate skew and hide hardware stalls, SRDatalog further employs root-level histogram-guided load balancing, structural helper-relation splitting, and stream-aligned rule multiplexing. On real-world program-analysis workloads, SRDatalog achieves geometric-mean speedups of 21x to 47x.

</details>


### [431] [Pre-Execution Query Slot-Time Prediction in Cloud Data Warehouses: A Feature-Scoped Machine Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20145)
*Prashant Kumar Pathak*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper proposes a machine learning model to predict BigQuery slot-time costs using pre-execution signals, achieving notable accuracy improvements over baseline methods, but encounters limitations with long-tail query predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-tenant cloud environments, query cost estimation is challenging due to variable slot-time usage and static query-planner heuristics failing to account for complex SQL structures, data skew, and workload contention.

Method: The method uses a HistGradientBoostingRegressor trained on structured features (e.g., SQL complexity, data volume, workload metadata) and textual features processed via a TF-IDF + TruncatedSVD pipeline. Predictive modeling excludes runtime factors to focus on pre-execution observable signals.

Result: The model achieves a 30-37% improvement in mean absolute error (MAE) on cost-significant queries compared to baseline methods. However, it fails to improve performance on long-tail queries dominated by unobserved runtime factors.

Conclusion: The feature-scoped ML approach proves effective for mid-range query cost prediction, but long-tail queries require additional feature exploration or methodologies. Future work could address these limitations via complexity-routed architectures or alternative strategies.

Abstract: Cloud data warehouses bill compute based on slot-time consumed. In shared multi-tenant environments, query cost is highly variable and hard to estimate before execution, causing budget overruns and degraded scheduling. Static query-planner heuristics fail to capture complex SQL structure, data skew, and workload contention. We present a feature-scoped machine learning approach that predicts BigQuery slot-time before execution using only pre-execution observable signals: a structured query complexity score derived from SQL operator costs, data volume features from planner estimates and workload metadata, and textual features from query text. We deliberately exclude runtime factors (slot-pool utilization, cache state, realized skew) unknowable at submission. The model uses a HistGradientBoostingRegressor trained on log-transformed slot-time, with a TF-IDF + TruncatedSVD-512 text pipeline fused with numeric and categorical features. Trained on 749 queries across seven deployment environments and evaluated out-of-distribution on 746 queries from two held-out environments, the model achieves MAE 1.17 slot-minutes, RMSE 4.71, and 74% explained variance on the full workload. On cost-significant queries (slot-time >= 0.01 min, N=282) the model achieves MAE 3.10 versus 4.95 for a predict-mean baseline and 4.54 for predict-median, a 30-37% reduction. On long-tail queries (>= 20 min, N=22) the model does not outperform trivial baselines, consistent with the hypothesis that long-tail queries are dominated by unobserved runtime factors outside the current feature scope. A complexity-routed dual-model architecture is described as a practical refinement, and directions for closing the long-tail gap are identified as future work.

</details>


<div id='physics.chem-ph'></div>

# physics.chem-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [432] [Improving Molecular Force Fields with Minimal Temporal Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19806)
*Ali Mollahosseini,Mohammed Haroon Dupty,Wee Sun Lee*

Main category: physics.chem-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces FRAMES, a novel training strategy leveraging minimal temporal information from MD simulations to significantly improve molecular energy and force prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance AI models' ability to accurately predict energies and forces for molecular systems using insights from Molecular Dynamics (MD) data.

Method: The method involves using an auxiliary loss function in a novel training strategy (FRAMES) that exploits temporal relationships within MD trajectories, relying minimally on consecutive frame pairs.

Result: FRAMES shows significant accuracy improvement in energy and force prediction, outperforming its Equiformer baseline on MD17 and ISO17 benchmarks.

Conclusion: FRAMES demonstrates that minimal temporal data from MD trajectories can improve performance effectively, suggesting that more temporal data may lead to redundancy and degraded results.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of energy and forces for 3D molecular systems is one of fundamental challenges at the core of AI for Science applications. Many powerful and data-efficient neural networks predict molecular energies and forces from single atomic configurations. However, one crucial aspect of the data generation process is rarely considered while learning these models i.e. Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulation. MD simulations generate time-ordered trajectories of atomic positions that fluctuate in energy and explore regions of the potential energy surface (e.g., under standard NVE/NVT ensembles), rather than being constructed to steadily lower the potential energy toward a minimum as in geometry relaxations. This work explores a novel way to leverage MD data, when available, to improve the performance of such predictors. We introduce a novel training strategy called FRAMES, that use an auxiliary loss function for exploiting the temporal relationships within MD trajectories. Counter-intuitively, on two atomistic benchmarks and a synthetic system we observe that minimal temporal information, captured by pairs of just two consecutive frames, is often sufficient to obtain the best performance, while adding longer trajectory sequences can introduce redundancy and degrade performance. On the widely used MD17 and ISO17 benchmarks, FRAMES significantly outperforms its Equiformer baseline, achieving highly competitive results in both energy and force accuracy. Our work not only presents a novel training strategy which improves the accuracy of the model, but also provides evidence that for distilling physical priors of atomic systems, more temporal data is not always better.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [433] [Forecasting Individual NetFlows using a Predictive Masked Graph Autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20483)
*Georgios Anyfantis,Pere Barlet-Ros*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Graph Neural Network (GNN) model to predict network flow-level traffic by modeling graph structures and connection features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to explore the capability of GNNs in accurately predicting NetFlow data by leveraging graph structures and connection features.

Method: The authors use sliding windows to divide network traffic into heterogeneous bidirectional graphs with IP, Port, and Connection nodes, then apply the GNN to model graph evolution and features.

Result: The approach demonstrates superior results in identifying Ports and IPs for connections, while feature reconstruction remains competitive with strong forecasting baselines.

Conclusion: The study highlights the potential of GNNs for accurate per-flow NetFlow traffic prediction.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a proof-of-concept Graph Neural Network model that can successfully predict network flow-level traffic (NetFlow) by accurately modelling the graph structure and the connection features. We use sliding-windows to split the network traffic in equal-sized heterogeneous bidirectional graphs containing IP, Port, and Connection nodes. We then use the GNN to model the evolution of the graph structure and the connection features. Our approach shows superior results when identifying the Port and IP to which connections attach, while feature reconstruction remains competitive with strong forecasting baselines. Overall, our work showcases the use of GNNs for per-flow NetFlow prediction.

</details>


<div id='physics.ao-ph'></div>

# physics.ao-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [434] [Mechanistic Interpretability Tool for AI Weather Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20467)
*Kirsten I. Tempest,Matthias Beylich,George C. Craig*

Main category: physics.ao-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces an open-source tool for interpreting AI-based weather models through mechanistic interpretability, demonstrating its potential with case studies on GraphCast.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance trust in AI weather models by understanding their prediction mechanisms, addressing the challenge of their black-box nature.

Method: An open-source tool integrates mechanistic interpretability concepts, using techniques like cosine similarity and PCA, to analyze latent representations in AI models.

Result: The tool effectively identified latent space directions linked to meteorological features in the GraphCast neural network.

Conclusion: The tool aids in deciphering AI weather models by uncovering interpretable features, supporting their broader acceptance and credibility.

Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) weather models are improving rapidly, and their forecasts are already competitive with long-established traditional Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP). To build confidence in this new methodology, it is critical that we understand how these predictions are generated. This is a huge challenge as these AI weather models remain largely black boxes. In other areas of Machine Learning (ML), mechanistic interpretability has emerged as a framework for understanding ML predictions by analysing the building blocks responsible for them. Here we present an open-source, highly adaptable tool which incorporates concepts from mechanistic interpretability. The tool organises internal latent representations from the model processor and allows for initial analyses, including cosine similarity and Principal Component Analysis (PCA), enabling the user to identify directions in latent space potentially associated with meteorological features. Applying our tool to the graph neural network GraphCast, we present preliminary case studies for mid-latitude synoptic-scale waves and specific humidity. These demonstrate the tool's ability to identify linear combinations of latent channels that appear to correspond to interpretable features.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [435] [Seeing Further and Wider: Joint Spatio-Temporal Enlargement for Micro-Video Popularity Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20311)
*Dali Wang,Yunyao Zhang,Junqing Yu,Yi-Ping Phoebe Chen,Chen Xu,Zikai Song*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: A new method for micro-video popularity prediction unifies spatio-temporal enlargement to better predict a video's popularity by using a novel frame scoring module and a scalable topology-aware memory bank.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches to micro-video popularity prediction face limitations in understanding long-term temporal dynamics and efficiently utilizing historical video relevance, which impacts prediction accuracy and efficiency.

Method: The paper introduces a temporal enlargement method using frame scoring for sparse sampling and dense perception, paired with a topology-aware memory bank for hierarchical clustering of historical videos to achieve scalable and efficient memory utilization.

Result: Experiments on three MVPP benchmarks show the proposed method outperforms 11 baseline models in metrics for prediction accuracy and ranking consistency.

Conclusion: The proposed unified framework improves the accuracy and scalability of micro-video popularity prediction via enhanced temporal analysis and an expandable memory system, offering substantial benefits for real-world applications.

Abstract: Micro-video popularity prediction (MVPP) aims to forecast the future popularity of videos on online media, which is essential for applications such as content recommendation and traffic allocation. In real-world scenarios, it is critical for MVPP approaches to understand both the temporal dynamics of a given video (temporal) and its historical relevance to other videos (spatial). However, existing approaches sufer from limitations in both dimensions: temporally, they rely on sparse short-range sampling that restricts content perception; spatially, they depend on flat retrieval memory with limited capacity and low efficiency, hindering scalable knowledge utilization. To overcome these limitations, we propose a unified framework that achieves joint spatio-temporal enlargement, enabling precise perception of extremely long video sequences while supporting a scalable memory bank that can infinitely expand to incorporate all relevant historical videos. Technically, we employ a Temporal Enlargement driven by a frame scoring module that extracts highlight cues from video frames through two complementary pathways: sparse sampling and dense perception. Their outputs are adaptively fused to enable robust long-sequence content understanding. For Spatial Enlargement, we construct a Topology-Aware Memory Bank that hierarchically clusters historically relevant content based on topological relationships. Instead of directly expanding memory capacity, we update the encoder features of the corresponding clusters when incorporating new videos, enabling unbounded historical association without unbounded storage growth. Extensive experiments on three widely used MVPP benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms 11 strong baselines across mainstream metrics, achieving robust improvements in both prediction accuracy and ranking consistency.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [436] [SAKE: Self-aware Knowledge Exploitation-Exploration for Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20146)
*Jielong Tang,Xujie Yuan,Jiayang Liu,Jianxing Yu,Xiao Dong,Lin Chen,Yunlai Teng,Shimin Di,Jian Yin*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces SAKE, a framework for improving Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (GMNER) on social media by combining internal and external knowledge effectively to handle challenging entity recognition and localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches to GMNER struggle with evolving, unseen entities on social media due to noisy external retrieval or limitations of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). A better method is needed to balance internal and external knowledge use effectively.

Method: The proposed SAKE framework utilizes a two-stage approach. The first stage involves Difficulty-aware Search Tag Generation to quantify uncertainty and construct a Chain-of-Thought dataset for self-awareness. The second stage uses agentic reinforcement learning to train the model for selective and effective external knowledge retrieval.

Result: SAKE demonstrates significant improvements in accuracy and effectiveness over existing methods in experiments conducted on two social media benchmarks.

Conclusion: The SAKE framework addresses the challenges in GMNER by successfully integrating internal reasoning and external exploration, achieving better performance and self-aware decision-making.

Abstract: Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (GMNER) aims to extract named entities and localize their visual regions within image-text pairs, serving as a pivotal capability for various downstream applications. In open-world social media platforms, GMNER remains challenging due to the prevalence of long-tailed, rapidly evolving, and unseen entities. To tackle this, existing approaches typically rely on either external knowledge exploration through heuristic retrieval or internal knowledge exploitation via iterative refinement in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, heuristic retrieval often introduces noisy or conflicting evidence that degrades precision on known entities, while solely internal exploitation is constrained by the knowledge boundaries of MLLMs and prone to hallucinations. To address this, we propose SAKE, an end-to-end agentic framework that harmonizes internal knowledge exploitation and external knowledge exploration via self-aware reasoning and adaptive search tool invocation. We implement this via a two-stage training paradigm. First, we propose Difficulty-aware Search Tag Generation, which quantifies the model's entity-level uncertainty through multiple forward samplings to produce explicit knowledge-gap signals. Based on these signals, we construct SAKE-SeCoT, a high-quality Chain-of-Thought dataset that equips the model with basic self-awareness and tool-use capabilities through supervised fine-tuning. Second, we employ agentic reinforcement learning with a hybrid reward function that penalizes unnecessary retrieval, enabling the model to evolve from rigid search imitation to genuine self-aware decision-making about when retrieval is truly necessary. Extensive experiments on two widely used social media benchmarks demonstrate SAKE's effectiveness.

</details>


### [437] [HaS: Accelerating RAG through Homology-Aware Speculative Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20452)
*Peng Peng,Weiwei Lin,Wentai Wu,Xinyang Wang,Yongheng Liu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: HaS proposes a framework to speed up retrieval in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems by leveraging query homology and speculative retrieval, reducing latency significantly while maintaining high accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of increasing retrieval latency in RAG systems due to growing knowledge databases, aiming to strike a balance between retrieval efficiency and accuracy.

Method: HaS employs a speculative retrieval mechanism on restricted scopes, followed by validation based on homologous query re-identification to bypass full-database retrieval when a query matches a previously observed homologous query.

Result: Experiments show HaS reduces retrieval latency by 23.74% and 36.99% across datasets with only a 1-2% decrease in accuracy.

Conclusion: HaS is an effective plug-and-play solution for accelerating retrieval in RAG systems, offering substantial efficiency gains especially for multi-hop queries, while maintaining near-optimal accuracy.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) expands the knowledge boundary of large language models (LLMs) at inference by retrieving external documents as context. However, retrieval becomes increasingly time-consuming as the knowledge databases grow in size. Existing acceleration strategies either compromise accuracy through approximate retrieval, or achieve marginal gains by reusing results of strictly identical queries. We propose HaS, a homology-aware speculative retrieval framework that performs low-latency speculative retrieval over restricted scopes to obtain candidate documents, followed by validating whether they contain the required knowledge. The validation, grounded in the homology relation between queries, is formulated as a homologous query re-identification task: once a previously observed query is identified as a homologous re-encounter of the incoming query, the draft is deemed acceptable, allowing the system to bypass slow full-database retrieval. Benefiting from the prevalence of homologous queries under real-world popularity patterns, HaS achieves substantial efficiency gains. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HaS reduces retrieval latency by 23.74% and 36.99% across datasets with only a 1-2% marginal accuracy drop. As a plug-and-play solution, HaS also significantly accelerates complex multi-hop queries in modern agentic RAG pipelines. Source code is available at: https://github.com/ErrEqualsNil/HaS.

</details>


### [438] [Self-Aware Vector Embeddings for Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Neuroscience-Inspired Framework for Temporal, Confidence-Weighted, and Relational Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20598)
*Naizhong Xu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces "SmartVector," a novel RAG framework that incorporates temporal awareness, confidence decay, and relational awareness into dense embeddings to address the limitations of conventional RAG systems in handling versioned and evolving queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional RAG systems lack consideration for temporal validity, trustworthiness, and dependency among embeddings, leading to inaccuracies in retrieval, especially for versioned and temporal-sensitive queries.

Method: SmartVector augments dense embeddings by adding explicitly modeled properties (temporal validity, confidence decay, relational awareness) and introduces a retrieval pipeline mixing multiple signals beyond cosine similarity. Lifecycle consolidation processes handle contradictions, updates, and memory reinforcement.

Result: SmartVector improved accuracy from 31.0% to 62.0%, reduced stale-answer rates, enhanced calibration, decreased embedding costs, and demonstrated robustness across high contradiction rates in a synthetic benchmark.

Conclusion: SmartVector significantly advances retrieval-based generation systems by addressing temporal and relational limitations, thereby enhancing accuracy, reducing errors, and optimizing computational costs.

Abstract: Modern retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems treat vector embeddings as static, context-free artifacts: an embedding has no notion of when it was created, how trustworthy its source is, or which other embeddings depend on it. This flattening of knowledge has a measurable cost: recent work on VersionRAG reports that conventional RAG achieves only 58% accuracy on versioned technical queries, because retrieval returns semantically similar but temporally invalid content. We propose SmartVector, a framework that augments dense embeddings with three explicit properties -- temporal awareness, confidence decay, and relational awareness -- and a five-stage lifecycle modeled on hippocampal-neocortical memory consolidation. A retrieval pipeline replaces pure cosine similarity with a four-signal score that mixes semantic relevance, temporal validity, live confidence, and graph-relational importance. A background consolidation agent detects contradictions, builds dependency edges, and propagates updates along those edges as graph-neural-network-style messages. Confidence is governed by a closed-form function combining an Ebbinghaus-style exponential decay, user-feedback reconsolidation, and logarithmic access reinforcement. We formalize the model, relate it to temporal knowledge graph embedding, agentic memory architectures, and uncertainty-aware RAG, and present a reference implementation. On a reproducible synthetic versioned-policy benchmark of 258 vectors and 138 queries, SmartVector roughly doubles top-1 accuracy over plain cosine RAG (62.0% vs. 31.0% on a held-out split), drops stale-answer rate from 35.0% to 13.3%, cuts Expected Calibration Error by nearly 2x (0.244 vs. 0.470), reduces re-embedding cost per single-word edit by 77%, and is robust across contradiction-injection rates from 0% to 75%.

</details>


### [439] [Semantic Recall for Vector Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20417)
*Leonardo Kuffo,Ioanna Tsakalidou,Roberta De Viti,Albert Angel,Jiří Iša,Rastislav Lenhardt*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'Semantic Recall' and 'Tolerant Recall' as new metrics to evaluate the quality of approximate nearest neighbor search algorithms, focusing on semantically relevant results rather than traditional recall methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional recall metrics penalize algorithms for not retrieving semantically irrelevant objects, which can be misleading. This paper aims to create a more accurate metric for evaluating retrieval quality by focusing only on semantically relevant objects.

Method: The authors propose Semantic Recall, which evaluates retrieval quality by considering semantically relevant results only, and Tolerant Recall, a proxy metric that approximates Semantic Recall for cases where semantic relevance cannot be determined.

Result: The proposed metrics are empirically demonstrated to better indicate retrieval quality and optimize search algorithms more effectively, achieving improved cost-quality tradeoffs.

Conclusion: Semantic Recall and Tolerant Recall provide a more precise measure of search quality, particularly in scenarios with limited relevant nearest neighbors, and optimizing algorithms for these metrics enhances performance.

Abstract: We introduce Semantic Recall, a novel metric to assess the quality of approximate nearest neighbor search algorithms by considering only semantically relevant objects that are theoretically retrievable via exact nearest neighbor search. Unlike traditional recall, semantic recall does not penalize algorithms for failing to retrieve objects that are semantically irrelevant to the query, even if those objects are among their nearest neighbors. We demonstrate that semantic recall is particularly useful for assessing retrieval quality on queries that have few relevant results among their nearest neighbors-a scenario we uncover to be common within embedding datasets. Additionally, we introduce Tolerant Recall, a proxy metric that approximates semantic recall when semantically relevant objects cannot be identified. We empirically show that our metrics are more effective indicators of retrieval quality, and that optimizing search algorithms for these metrics can lead to improved cost-quality tradeoffs.

</details>


### [440] [Coverage, Not Averages: Semantic Stratification for Trustworthy Retrieval Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20763)
*Andrew Klearman,Radu Revutchi,Rohin Garg,Rishav Chakravarti,Samuel Marc Denton,Yuan Xue*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper addresses biases in evaluating retrieval quality in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) by proposing a semantic stratification framework for improved and transparent evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current retrieval evaluation methods rely on heuristically constructed query sets, which introduce intrinsic biases that hinder accurate and robust assessments of retrieval quality.

Method: The paper formalizes retrieval evaluation as a statistical estimation problem and introduces semantic stratification, a method that organizes documents into entity-based clusters, ensures formal semantic coverage, and systematically generates queries for missing strata.

Result: Experiments demonstrate the identification of systematic coverage gaps, explanations for performance variance, and improvements in evaluation stability, transparency, and trustworthiness compared to aggregate metrics.

Conclusion: The proposed framework overcomes the limitations of existing retrieval evaluation methods by offering a semantically grounded, interpretable, and reliable approach to assess retrieval systems.

Abstract: Retrieval quality is the primary bottleneck for accuracy and robustness in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Current evaluation relies on heuristically constructed query sets, which introduce a hidden intrinsic bias. We formalize retrieval evaluation as a statistical estimation problem, showing that metric reliability is fundamentally limited by the evaluation-set construction. We further introduce \emph{semantic stratification}, which grounds evaluation in corpus structure by organizing documents into an interpretable global space of entity-based clusters and systematically generating queries for missing strata. This yields (1) formal semantic coverage guarantees across retrieval regimes and (2) interpretable visibility into retrieval failure modes.
  Experiments across multiple benchmarks and retrieval methods validate our framework. The results expose systematic coverage gaps, identify structural signals that explain variance in retrieval performance, and show that stratified evaluation yields more stable and transparent assessments while supporting more trustworthy decision-making than aggregate metrics.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [441] [Secure Rate-Distortion-Perception: A Randomized Distributed Function Computation Approach for Realism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20245)
*Gustaf Åhlgren,Onur Günlü*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This study formulates and analyzes secure rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-offs for applications like neural image compression, focusing on minimizing information leakage over communication channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address security risks in applications requiring perceptual quality maintenance for compressed data transmitted over public channels.

Method: Analytical characterization of secure RDP regions for noiseless and broadcast channels, employing tools like separate source-channel coding and random binning-based coding.

Result: Exact secure RDP regions are derived for noiseless channels, and an inner bound is established and tightened for broadcast channels. Common randomness significantly reduces communication rates in secure RDP scenarios.

Conclusion: Strong secrecy, low distortion, and high perceptual quality can be achieved simultaneously through random binning-based coding techniques.

Abstract: Fundamental rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-offs arise in applications requiring maintained perceptual quality of reconstructed data, such as neural image compression. When compressed data is transmitted over public communication channels, security risks emerge. We therefore study secure RDP under negligible information leakage over both noiseless channels and broadcast channels, BCs, with correlated noise components. For noiseless channels, the exact secure RDP region is characterized. For BCs, an inner bound is derived and shown to be tight for a class of more-capable BCs. Separate source-channel coding is further shown to be optimal for this exact secure RDP region with unlimited common randomness available. Moreover, when both encoder and decoder have access to side information correlated with the source and the channel is noiseless, the exact RDP region is established. If only the decoder has correlated side information in the noiseless setting, an inner bound is derived along with a special case where the region is exact. Binary and Gaussian examples demonstrate that common randomness can significantly reduce the communication rate in secure RDP settings, unlike in standard rate-distortion settings. Thus, our results illustrate that random binning-based coding achieves strong secrecy, low distortion, and high perceptual quality simultaneously.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [442] [CO$_2$ sequestration hybrid solver using isogeometric alternating-directions and collocation-based robust variational physics informed neural networks (IGA-ADS-CRVPINN)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20731)
*Askold Vilkha,Tomasz Służalec,Marcin Łoś,Maciej Paszyński*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a hybrid solver for simulating $CO_2$ behavior in porous structures by combining IGA-ADS and CRVPINN methods, achieving superior computational efficiency compared to traditional baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance efficiency and accuracy in simulating $CO_2$ sequestration dynamics in porous structures, as current methods can be computationally demanding.

Method: A hybrid approach combining IGA-ADS for saturation computation and CRVPINN for pressure computation, leveraging pretrained neural networks and Adam optimization for fast updates.

Result: The hybrid solver is over three times faster than the baseline method on a single computational node, demonstrating significant computational benefits.

Conclusion: The hybrid solver efficiently simulates $CO_2$ behavior in porous structures and shows promise for further advancements, including applications in $H_2$ storage and inverse problem-solving.

Abstract: This paper presents the hybrid solver for a $CO_2$ sequestration problem. The solver uses the IGA-ADS (IsoGeometric Analysis Alternating Directions solver) to compute the saturation scalar field update using the explicit method, and CRVPINN (Collocation-based Robust Variational Physics Informed Neural Networks solver) to compute the pressure scalar field. The study focuses on simulating the physical behavior of $CO_2$ in porous structures, excluding chemical reactions. The mathematical model is based on Darcy's Law. The CRVPINN is pretrained on the initial pressure configuration, and the time step pressure updates require only 100 iterations of the Adam method per time step. We compare our hybrid IGA-ADS solver, coupled with the CRVPINN method, with a baseline of the IGA-ADS solver coupled with the MUMPS direct solver. Our hybrid solver is over 3 times faster on a single computational node from the ARES cluster of ACK CYFRONET. Future work includes extensive testing, inverse problem solving, and potential application to $H_2$ storage problems.

</details>


### [443] [Machine learning moment closure models for the radiative transfer equation IV: enforcing symmetrizable hyperbolicity in two dimensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20143)
*Juntao Huang*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a gradient-based machine learning moment closure model to improve the radiative transfer equation (RTE) in 2D2V, ensuring hyperbolicity while preserving structural properties from the classical $P_N$ model. Numerical results show better performance than the previous models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to extend machine learning-based moment closure models for the radiative transfer equation to a more complex 2D2V setting, addressing hyperbolicity and leveraging structural properties of the classical $P_N$ model to improve accuracy and robustness.

Method: The researchers analyze the $P_N$ model, preserving its leading parts while modifying the highest-order block row. They introduce block-diagonal symmetrizers and derive explicit algebraic conditions for symmetrizable hyperbolicity. These conditions guide the design of a data-driven closure with symmetric positive definite matrices and symmetric blocks, learned from data.

Result: The extended ML moment closure framework demonstrates improved accuracy compared to the classical $P_N$ model while maintaining hyperbolicity through its structural guarantees.

Conclusion: This work successfully extends the ML moment closure model to the more complex 2D2V RTE setting. By leveraging structural analysis of the $P_N$ model and enforcing hyperbolicity through a well-disciplined ML approach, the method ensures robustness and numerical improvements over traditional models.

Abstract: This is our fourth work in the series on machine learning (ML) moment closure models for the radiative transfer equation (RTE). In the first three papers of this series, we considered the RTE in slab geometry in 1D1V (i.e. one dimension in physical space and one dimension in angular space), and introduced a gradient-based ML moment closure [1], then enforced the hyperbolicity through a symmetrizer [2], or together with physical characteristic speeds by learning the eigenvalues of the Jacobian matrix [3].
  Here, we extend our framework to the RTE in 2D2V (i.e. two dimensions in physical space and two dimensions in angular space). The main idea is to preserve the leading part of the classical $P_N$ model and modify only the highest-order block row. By analyzing the structural properties of the $P_N$ model, we show that its coefficient matrices are symmetric and admit a block-tridiagonal structure. Then we use this property to introduce a block-diagonal symmetrizer for the ML moment model and derive explicit algebraic conditions on the closure blocks which guarantee the symmetrizable hyperbolicity of the resulting ML system. These conditions lead to a natural parametrization of the closure in terms of a symmetric positive definite matrix together with symmetric closure blocks, which can be learned from data while automatically enforcing symmetrizable hyperbolicity by construction. The numerical results show that the proposed framework improves upon the classical $P_N$ model while maintaining hyperbolicity.

</details>


<div id='cs.ET'></div>

# cs.ET [[Back]](#toc)

### [444] [What Makes a Bacterial Model a Good Reservoir Computer? Predicting Performance from Separability and Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19850)
*Laura Alonso Bartolomé,Jean-Loup Faulon,Xavier Hinaut*

Main category: cs.ET

TL;DR: This study explores bacterial metabolic models as computational substrates, finding they support nonlinear computation but with varying performance across species and mutants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biological systems naturally process environmental information, suggesting potential as computational substrates. This study seeks to validate bacterial metabolic models as candidates for reservoir computing.

Method: Using dynamic flux balance analysis (dFBA), researchers simulated bacterial and yeast growth dynamics, analyzed reservoir states, and tested computational performance on classification tasks using metrics like kernel and generalisation ranks.

Result: Several microbial metabolic models exhibited high classification accuracy, with species showing a trade-off between convergence speed and peak performance. E. coli mutants underperformed compared to the wild-type model.

Conclusion: Bacterial metabolic models have potential for reservoir computing, with findings guiding future identification of optimal microbial strains for experimental and computational uses.

Abstract: Biological systems are promising substrates for computation because they naturally process environmental information through complex internal dynamics. In this study, we investigate whether bacterial metabolic models can act as physical reservoirs and whether their computational performance can be predicted from dynamical properties linked to separability and similarity. We simulated the growth dynamics of five bacterial species, one yeast species, and 29 Escherichia coli single-gene deletion mutants using dynamic flux balance analysis (dFBA), with glucose and xylose concentrations as inputs and growth curves as reservoir states. Computational performance was assessed on random nonlinear classification tasks using a linear readout, while reservoir properties linked to separability and similarity were characterised through kernel and generalisation ranks computed from growth-curve state matrices. Several microbial models achieved high classification accuracy, showing that bacterial metabolic dynamics can support nonlinear computation. Clear differences were observed between species, with some models converging more rapidly and others reaching higher maximum accuracy, revealing a trade-off between convergence speed and peak performance. In contrast, all E. coli mutants were dominated by the wild-type model, suggesting that gene deletions reduce the dynamical richness required for efficient computation. The difference between kernel and generalisation ranks was generally associated with improved accuracy, but deviations across models and sensitivity at low rank values limited its predictive power in practice. Overall, these results show that bacterial metabolic models constitute promising substrates for reservoir computing and provide a first step towards identifying microbial strains with favourable computational properties for future experimental implementations.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [445] [scpFormer: A Foundation Model for Unified Representation and Integration of the Single-Cell Proteomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20003)
*Qifeng Zhou,Lei Yu,Yuzhi Guo,Yuwei Miao,Hehuan Ma,Wenliang Zhong,Lin Xu,Junzhou Huang*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: The paper introduces scpFormer, a transformer-based foundation model for single-cell proteomics that addresses challenges in data integration and biomarker discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The integration of single-cell proteomic data faces challenges due to fragmented and variable antibody panels, which limit scalability and precision oncology applications.

Method: scpFormer employs a transformer-based architecture that uses a continuous, sequence-anchored tokenization approach. It incorporates Evolutionary Scale Modeling (ESM) and value-aware expression embeddings for dynamic mapping of variable panels into a shared space.

Result: scpFormer effectively generates global cell representations that excel in large-scale batch integration and unsupervised clustering. It also enables in silico panel expansion and contributes to bulk-omics tasks like cancer drug response prediction.

Conclusion: scpFormer offers a scalable, panel-agnostic framework for single-cell proteomics, addressing data integration challenges and enhancing biomarker discovery for precision oncology.

Abstract: The integration of single-cell proteomic data is often hindered by the fragmented nature of targeted antibody panels. To address this limitation, we introduce scpFormer, a transformer-based foundation model designed for single-cell proteomics. Pre-trained on over 390 million cells, scpFormer replaces standard index-based tokenization with a continuous, sequence-anchored approach. By combining Evolutionary Scale Modeling (ESM) with value-aware expression embeddings, it dynamically maps variable panels into a shared semantic space without artificial discretization. We demonstrate that scpFormer generates global cell representations that perform competitively in large-scale batch integration and unsupervised clustering. Moreover, its open-vocabulary architecture facilitates in silico panel expansion, assisting in the reconstruction of biological manifolds in sparse clinical datasets. Finally, this learned protein co-expression logic is transferable to bulk-omics tasks, supporting applications like cancer drug response prediction. scpFormer provides a versatile, panel-agnostic framework to facilitate scalable biomarker discovery and precision oncology.

</details>


### [446] [AROMA: Augmented Reasoning Over a Multimodal Architecture for Virtual Cell Genetic Perturbation Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20263)
*Zhenyu Wang,Geyan Ye,Wei Liu,Man Tat Alexander Ng*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: The paper introduces AROMA, a novel framework combining multimodal information for improved predictions in virtual cell genetic perturbation modeling, delivering enhanced accuracy and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for virtual cell genetic perturbation modeling face issues such as unconstrained reasoning, weak alignment with regulatory topology, and lack of interpretability in predictions.

Method: AROMA integrates textual evidence, graph-topology information, and protein sequence features and adopts a two-stage optimization strategy for training.

Result: Experiments reveal that AROMA outperforms existing methods across multiple cell lines, shows robustness in zero-shot and challenging scenarios, and provides reliable and interpretable predictions.

Conclusion: AROMA represents a significant advancement by combining knowledge-driven multimodal modeling and evidence retrieval, offering a robust approach for virtual cell perturbation prediction.

Abstract: Virtual cell modeling predicts molecular state changes under genetic perturbations in silico, which is essential for biological mechanism studies. However, existing approaches suffer from unconstrained reasoning, uninterpretable predictions, and retrieval signals that are weakly aligned with regulatory topology. To address these limitations, we propose AROMA, an Augmented Reasoning Over a Multimodal Architecture for virtual cell genetic perturbation modeling. AROMA integrates textual evidence, graph-topology information, and protein sequence features to model perturbation-target dependencies, and is trained with a two-stage optimization strategy to yield predictions that are both accurate and interpretable. We also construct two knowledge graphs and a perturbation reasoning dataset, PerturbReason, containing more than 498k samples, as reusable resources for the virtual cell domain. Experiments show that AROMA outperforms existing methods across multiple cell lines, and remains robust under zero-shot evaluation on an unseen cell line, as well as in knowledge-sparse, long-tail scenarios. Overall, AROMA demonstrates that combining knowledge-driven multimodal modeling with evidence retrieval provides a promising pathway toward more reliable and interpretable virtual cell perturbation prediction. Model weights are available at https://huggingface.co/blazerye/AROMA. Code is available at https://github.com/blazerye/AROMA.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [447] [Do Small Language Models Know When They're Wrong? Confidence-Based Cascade Scoring for Educational Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19781)
*Tyler Burleigh*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: The study explores using verbalized confidence in small LMs for routing tasks in cascade systems for scoring educational work. It identifies confidence discrimination as the key factor affecting accuracy, cost, and latency trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for scalable, accurate, and cost-effective automated scoring systems, especially for student-AI math conversation evaluations.

Method: Cascade systems leveraging verbalized confidence as a routing signal were tested, using model pairs GPT-5.4, Claude 4.5+, and Gemini 3.1 with expert-scored data.

Result: Small LMs varied in confidence discrimination, impacting system effectiveness. Strong confidence variance yielded cascades with minimal accuracy loss, while near-degenerate confidence distributions failed to bridge the accuracy gap.

Conclusion: Effective verbalized confidence enables cost and latency reduction while maintaining large-LM accuracy. Confidence discrimination is central to successful implementation of cascade systems.

Abstract: Automated scoring of student work at scale requires balancing accuracy against cost and latency. In "cascade" systems, small language models (LMs) handle easier scoring tasks while escalating harder ones to larger LMs -- but the challenge is determining which cases to escalate. We explore verbalized confidence -- asking the LM to state a numerical confidence alongside its prediction -- as a routing signal. Using 2,100 expert-scored decisions from student-AI math conversations, we evaluate cascade systems built from GPT-5.4, Claude 4.5+, and Gemini 3.1 model pairs. We find that: (1) confidence discrimination varies widely across small LMs, with the best achieving AUROC 0.857 and the worst producing a near-degenerate confidence distribution; (2) confidence tracks human scoring difficulty, with lower LM confidence where annotators disagreed and took longer to score; (3) the best cascade approached large-LM accuracy (kappa 0.802 vs. 0.819) at 76% lower cost and 61% lower latency. Confidence discrimination is the bottleneck: the two small LMs with meaningful confidence variance yielded cascades with no statistically detectable kappa loss, while the third -- whose confidence was near-degenerate -- could not close the accuracy gap regardless of threshold. Small LMs with strong discrimination let practitioners trade cost for accuracy along the frontier; those without it do not.

</details>


### [448] [Model Capability Assessment and Safeguards for Biological Weaponization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19811)
*Michael Richter*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper examines the operational intelligence and moderation of advanced AI models to evaluate their vulnerabilities to biological misuse, highlighting Gemini's contextual limitations and providing policy guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To benchmark the capabilities and safety measures of advanced AI models to assess their potential for misuse in biological applications.

Method: The study used a two-phase testing approach with benign STEM tasks followed by subtle harmful intent prompts; weaponization analysis focused on Gemini's safeguards across four access modes.

Result: Gemini demonstrated high operational intelligence but lacked adequate moderation mechanisms, leading to concerning misuse scenarios in specific access environments.

Conclusion: Actionable policy and guidance are necessary to mitigate misuse risks, particularly as AI outputs may gain regulatory consideration as technical data.

Abstract: AI leaders and safety reports increasingly warn that advances in model reasoning may enable biological misuse, including by low-expertise users, while major labs describe safeguards as expanding but still evolving rather than settled. This study benchmarks ChatGPT 5.2 Auto, Gemini 3 Pro Thinking, Claude Opus 4.5 and Meta's Muse Spark Thinking on 73 novice-framed, open-ended benign STEM prompts to measure operational intelligence. On benign quantitative tasks, both Gemini and meta scored very high; ChatGPT was partially useful but text-thinned, and Claude was sparsest with some apparent false-positive refusals. A second test set detected subtle harmful intent: edge case prompts revealed Gemini's seeming lack of contextual awareness. These results warranted a focused weaponization analysis on Gemini as capability appeared to be outpacing moderation calibration. Gemini was tested across four access environments and reported cases include poison-ivy-to-crowded-transit escalation, poison production and extraction via international-anonymous logged-out AI Mode, and other concerning examples. Biological misuse may become more prevalent as a geopolitical tool, increasing the urgency of U.S. policy responses, especially if model outputs come to be treated as regulated technical data. Guidance is provided for 25 high-risk agents to help distinguish legitimate use cases from higher-risk ones.

</details>


### [449] [Bias in the Tails: How Name-conditioned Evaluative Framing in Resume Summaries Destabilizes LLM-based Hiring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.19984)
*Huy Nghiem,Phuong-Anh Nguyen-Le,Sy-Tuyen Ho,Hal Daume*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: This paper examines racial and gender bias in candidate summaries generated by LLMs in hiring processes, using nearly a million controlled experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to study the subtle biases that large language models introduce into candidate assessment pipelines, especially in a setup where they construct summaries of resumes.

Method: Researchers use a large-scale controlled experiment with synthetic resumes and real-world job postings, systematically perturbing race-gender identifiers in names to measure LLM output consistency.

Result: Factual content in summaries remains stable, but evaluative language varies slightly based on name-conditioned biases, which are more apparent in open-source models at the extremes of assessments.

Conclusion: While traditional fairness audits might not detect instability in LLM outputs, the study uncovers pathways for automation bias in hiring simulations through LLM-generated summaries.

Abstract: Research has documented LLMs' name-based bias in hiring and salary recommendations.
